speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number three. In 1939, before the Mossad, you joined the Haganah. How did that come about?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's no one who didn't join Haganah or Etzel or Lehi. It was very traditional that in the school always, some teacher is always-- he wasn't Haganah, or Etzel, or this. So the one teacher called me at the time. I knew that some others were called and all this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So already, he called me to be-- how do you say-- in, in the Haganah. And I said yes. It was-- I think that it was very normal for any youngster to be in Haganah. He couldn't be even-- because all his friends had been there earlier or later. That was traditional. So it was-- you know, pass letters. You clean the, the weapons. You study Morse, semiphore. That was normal. But at that time, you were about 10 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: 12, I would say.

speaker: interviewee
text: About 12, you know? It, it comes to give you into a new feeling. You knew that it happens all the time. This guy was called, you know, secret and all this-- and until then, you know? So I would say it was only, you know, this kind of thing, how to, to study for the future. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at this time as well, you were-- you said you seriously started studying painting when you were about 16. Were you now painting? And were you doing--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was painting from time to time, mostly drawing-- in the beginning, only drawing, a lot of drawing. And also, I made a, a few exhibitions only for drawing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That, I've done a lot. And from time to time, paint. And then later-- I would say when I was 22 years-- I studied more in Vienna at the closest-- you know, they were interrupted. Because I was already working. Then, then I made it about one year as painting.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at what point did you join the Mossad?

speaker: interviewee
text: After I finished the, the high technical school. It's about four years. I would say after war-- after high technical school, and then I went to the army. And because I was known in the high technical school as good in technology and I like locks. I like safes. I liked explosives and all this-- in the Haganah, I already studied explosives, you know-- how do you say-- anti-explosives to be-- to protect yourself from explosives, and also, how to make explosives, how to make mines and all these kind of-- how do you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Demolition?

speaker: interviewee
text: Booby traps and all these kinds of things. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then I-- there was a problem there, a technical problem that they had. When they went to the school or back to the Technion that I studied already, didn't finish as an engineer-- they said, he can solve you the problem-- the secret service-- and he can solve your problem. It was something-- you know, through the, through the war was something-- say, it was mined, and also couldn't open it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I helped it-- helped to take away the mine, you know, the booby trap, and open it. And then they said, why shouldn't you continue to work with us? I said yes. I never thought about that I'm going to be the secret service. So my mother, she wants me to be an engineer. So I, I went to the high technical school. And she said it's all-- it looks like that she helped me to be in the secret service, in a way. So that's what happened. But you couldn't tell anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't tell her. You know, you don't tell your Jewish mother that you went to the secret service. After what all happened to our family, there's no way you can tell her. Because she always would be very worried. Where are you going? Where-- so I didn't tell-- never told her.

speaker: interviewer
text: She thought that I'm working in a way with the government, but other things. You know, where, where are you located? I said, you know, on the Mount Carmel or my office and all this. But she never knew that I'm secret service. Were you not allowed to tell, or did you just choose?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not allowed. Normally, it's not a question to allow-- to be allowed. You don't tell it normally to-- if I would tell, and I know my mother, if I tell her, she wouldn't let me work. She would all  the time ask questions-- where, what's the danger, and all this. Secondly, normally, people tell their, their very near surroundings-- your father, your mother, your-- no, your wife. Children, no, because they're too young to understand. Only when they are big, they already-- it's impossible. They wouldn't understand in the secret service, don't tell what you're doing. So with my mother, I never tried, even, to tell her. And also, my family-- I never told them that I'm the secret service.

speaker: interviewer
text: Being an artist already at the time, you, you also became very proficient at disguising and, and makeup. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say that was not exactly with this. When I went into the secret service, it came to my mind that if you have very few people, OK, and you want to make them more, I say. So I looked at it. Why is it that I walk here, I can't walk again, because he knows me already? Let's say you stay someplace for five hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Said, what, how could you do, what can you do with yourself that you will be changed? So I start to think about your appearance? And what could I change that your, your appearance will, will be changed? And that's why it brought me to, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the disguising. So I, I built-- it was the first time to build in the Secret Service a department of, of disguising. So there was a lot of clothes, a lot of things, all this, [? paruks ?] and all. Then I went-- a lot of, a lot of time took, took me to-- well, to change teeth. Because they change a lot the form of the face. And also, other kind of teeth, they change you of a voice. It was very important.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I worked with some dentist, a doctor, for many years to work. And we disguised people that they can even cross a border and to meet people. And they wouldn't know. My exam-- then I went out, built all this, this unit that they have to go to their wife or go to their girls or go to their date, go to their relatives, and they wouldn't be recognized. If he was done it, he passed the exam. He didn't it, he couldn't work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And also, I made a lot of times that they-- let's say one of my students went-- he went to the secret service and said-- I lost my identity. No. I found an identity. Whose is it? It's his identity. And he said, oh, thank you. I said, no, no. You have to pay me with some money. What is it? I just did it for nothing? And he, he himself-- the Secret Service didn't recognize him. So that was-- how do you say-- something that you have to make exams on your near people to, to make sure that it is not you. Because you have a feeling when you are disguised that you are yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I went to my girl-- I had a girlfriend. She is now in Los Angeles. I was make a date. Well, I stood beside her and said, what are you doing here, and all this talk to her. She didn't want to talk to me. So she's in Los Angeles. She remind me. Then the story about my mother before I went.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it, it's a big, a big work, and a big thinking of details, and a big thinking of your appearance and what, what can change. So I thought about the face, about your, about your appearance, about the way you walk. It's like, it's like acting. You don't have to remind, even by your walking, that it's you. You understand? So what makes you appear like what you are? And I made a lot of exercise in work that you stay in one place-- let's say 24 hours-- by the same people, and they don't know you are-- that you are the same man. That's what [? I-- ?] The, the, the incident with your mother is particularly interesting, because-- if you could tell us that story. Because it also--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I went to the-- to the [INAUDIBLE]? OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Because it, it had to do with your sister. And you said that you always didn't want to hear about the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then you did. Can you tell us this?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Well, normally, as I said, when I was a child, I was afraid from the word Holocaust. I didn't want to hear anymore. Because I didn't-- I felt frustrated. Nobody can do anything about happened about the past. And suddenly, I was chosen one of the team-- I was not alone-- one of the team to capture physically Eichmann, to disguise him, and to hold him as much as needed. That was my, my role in the team's role. I describe everybody as another role.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I have to go, I said to myself, to hell with you. Till now, you didn't-- you don't know what happened to your sister. You don't know who, who your sister was. You don't know about her children. You never heard one story. And you are going now to capture Eichmann?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe you want to ask him a question. Maybe it will be important if you will go now and to hear the, the story. And I said to myself, I'm going to my mother. Said, no, I want to hear about the Holocaust. No way it will work out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I was interviewed by a journalist. They said it was cruel to go to your mother and disguise yourself. I said, what was so cruel? In our family, we-- I, I made a lot of these kinds of things-- coming in, disappear, come. I was not the only one. I tell in the, in the, in the, in the stories what's connected to the Holocaust. I said to myself, I have to come in. How I'm going to do it? I said, I'm going to disguise myself as an American. So I, I dressed myself and all this. And I was convinced that I'm OK. I did-- from-- between me and myself, I never ask anybody. And normally, I would go every Friday-- I would go to see my mother when I was in Israel. OK? I was-- lived in Tel Aviv and she was in Haifa.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went with my Falcon, American Ford. I put myself there. She was looking on me on the terrace, waiting and waving. She was happy that I eat with her. You know, she lit the candles on Sabbath This time, I left my, my car away. She was standing. And I was another guy, like another. I have, you know, as always, you have to have a cover story, what you're doing and all this. And I go up. I see her. She's looking, looking. I go up, I knock on the door. She says oh, Peter. [INAUDIBLE], they call me sometimes. So then she opens the door. And she looked at me. You know, even as much as you disguise yourself are you sure from all the exercises, you're never sure. Because you in yourself, you know, you are the man. And she is my mother. And she would say, would, hello. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said, excuse me, who are you? I said, I am the friend of Peter. And she said to me, really? What are you doing here? I came to rent a room, because I'm going to study in the Technion. She said, a room, here? Who told you this? Said, Peter. She said, oh, my Peter. I know what room. But you know what? He's coming in a while, because he told me he's coming. So let's sit down in the, the room. And I'm going to give you some gefilte fish and some tea. You sit here and wait for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a big, large corridor. She went to the kitchen. And she disappeared. I said that's the time. I look at the painting. I look at the pictures. I look at the wall. You know, the Eastern Europeans, they like to put on the wall all the-- even Americans like sometimes to put a lot of pictures of their children, all this. That was hanging on the wall and under the glass.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see, I see my sister, my, my brothers, and all this. And I said-- I look at the children. And I said, you know, at the time, they dressed like, you know, paintings-- big hat, long-- no, not fitted. And I said to [INAUDIBLE], why didn't you ask till now nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: And suddenly, I hear my mother walking. So I just stand like this. And she said-- she put the fish and all this on the table. And she said to me, this is the sister. And these are the children, the relative. And this is [INAUDIBLE] and all this. She gives me names. And she, and she starts to tell me the story. And then she said, but one thing. You do me a favor. Never tell Peter that you talk with me or I talk to you about the Holocaust, about the sister. He doesn't like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, no, no, I promise you. And all the time, she went out to the terrace. And she said, I don't know what happened. Almost Shabbat, and I have to light the candle, and he's not here. I said, how are we going to finish it? So I had to finish it only by one thing, that I have to do something. So she went to the terrace. I went behind her. I hugged her strongly. I took off my wig, my glasses, my teeth, and all this. She looked at me and said, oh, Peter, one day you're going to kill me. I know you're going to kill me. Why do you do it to me? I said, because you know, I just make a joke. You know, what is the big thing? No, you-- these jokes, terrible. And suddenly she said-- and she, you know, fight me like this in the room. And I laugh. And she start also to laugh and said, what is all this stories about the Holocaust, about your sister, and all this? What happened now? Where are you going? I said, to Paris. She said, to Paris? You go to Paris? Never, never believed me.

words.text: light the candle, and he's not
start also to laugh and said,

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I tell you, I'm going to Paris. I call you. You know, I looked her in eyes. And I said, you know, it's not easy to lie to a mother. I said, but a Polish Jewish mother, forget it. You never lie to her. It's always-- they look at your eyes. You said, OK, OK, OK. But remember, the refrigerator is full of food. And don't go for long time and call me. And every call I called her, she asked me, don't forget that the, the refrigerator is full of food.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, what do you think-- in, in Paris, they don't eat? Oh, but like your food of your mother, you never can find. So that's the story about recognizing better the Holocaust through my disguise to my mother. You had, you had been in the, in the Mossad for quite a while. You were pretty much an operative.

speaker: interviewee
text: 28 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: 28 Years.

speaker: interviewee
text: 28 Years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were an operative, and you worked out--

speaker: interviewee
text: Operating, yes, most of my time. I was-- in the end, I was the head of the operation, head of the operation in the Mossad.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the end, you were the head of the Mossad, head of operation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at the end, much more than the end-- a few years, about seven years. Most of the time, I was only in operation.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the first time that you heard the name Adolf Eichmann?

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard about it-- I would say I heard a lot of it, I think, even '45.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was-- in our family, Eichmann wasn't known. This was not not a man that he was not known. But the most came to me when I was in the secret service. I knew that people look after Eichmann. There was some people, they look after Eichmann, so it was look. From 1950 that I was there till 1960, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I all the time knew that people look after him. And, and I didn't know what's the, what's the results, only when I was asked to join this mission. I was not ever, ever-- people make mistakes sometimes-- I will never knew about-- how do you say-- I'm not a-- how you call it-- Nazi hunter. I just-- that was one of my operations for a month. And I work the Mossad 28 years. So people remember it, because it was a big, big, heroic act. And they justify it. But remember, other things are, you know, are not known, as it was now revealed only in these days, about two weeks ago, that I was the man who brought the scientists-- brought the names and the documents of the scientist who, after the war, want to kill again Israel through-- they want to build the missiles in Egypt. I was the man who, who revealed it and brought it, and brought it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was open now again. Some people called-- oh, we thought only you capture, capture Eichmann. So, so you see? After a time-- 30 years-- some people, they bring it to, to the news. How did that come about that you were chosen? And, and do you remember the story of how that whole-- when they first--

speaker: interviewee
text: How I was chosen? So somebody had to be chosen then. Accidentally, it was me. I would say it was the-- Ben-Gurion, the head of state, decided and gave the order to capture Eichmann from the beginning, or from the beginning, from '45, even was the head of the Jewish agency then. And later, '48, he was the head of the Jewish state. That was an order stand all the time to capture Eichmann. I was in the secret service. And then because in 1960, I already was an operate of 10 years. I had the experience. I could appear with a foreign language, a foreign passport. I was-- had a black belt in-- how do you call it-- in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Karate.

speaker: interviewee
text: High karate. I was a master of disguise. It was important. And that was enough to choose me as the one that physically to capture him, to disguise him, and till-- and to sit with him till we bring him to Israel. And because I was, I say, familiar with operation, I-- and I have to capture him, it, it was normal that the plan pursued me [INAUDIBLE] to capture him. That's why I also did the planning of it. And I was not alone. There was a team of seven, eight people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Seven or eight people were on the team?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: People you've--

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody had his role. One was technician. One was, say, the one who knew all, all the way, the airport, and and this. One was to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was the renting of the, of the cars and, and the houses. One was at the head of the whole operation. When I capture him, I can't be, you know, also-- how do you say-- the captain and also to be the one who capture him. Has-- somebody has to coordinate all this. And Isser Harel, himself, the head of the secret service, was there-- not in the act of but far away, that he got the information before the capture and after  the capture. There was a doctor also for sure, and a woman that she was in the room, you know, that will show we-- like after we captured him, to hold the-- to him in the room. And there will be-- how do you say-- a woman and a man. One rented it that looks like it's a normal house there. And we had about nine cars, eight cars, a lot of houses that we didn't know where we were going to hide him if there would be problems with the police. If they ran-- let's say they capture us. After the capture, you never know how it will come out, and-- how do you say-- the we can find any direction where they have a house. Through, you know, the transportation, through the way we take him, we didn't know how we take him-- by, by air or by water or by ship, by ship, I said, by water, and by train, by car. We didn't know. We just decided to capture him and then to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, it seemed that, in reading the book, that when you-- in capturing him, you, you actually had no idea after you captured him how you want to get him out of the country at that point.

speaker: interviewee
text: No way, we didn't know. And that was only, only decided because accidentally-- really accidentally, there was 150 years of the independence of the, of South America and also, I would say, Argentina. In Buenos Aires, there was a lot of celebration, and all this. And it was a good--

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to talk about that as we--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we get to that part.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: The, the the plan was-- you first actually told your mother you were going to Paris. And in fact, you were going to Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said I'm going to Paris. But the, the real way was to go is to go to really to Argentina. But in-- one, one of the exercises we did was I say to-- you have to be sure that this capture will be for sure. I said, nobody will see it. And we can transport him to Israel. Even we had, you know other alternative-- let's say the-- if the police or somebody notice the act, that we will, will not release him. We will come to the police and say this the Eichmann. And all the newspapers around the world will talk about it. There could be no way that he will escape trial.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a lot. You know, like every operation has to be alternative if it doesn't succeed, succeed, any way to do it. So-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and we had a chance that was, as I said before, the independence of South America and Argentina, that we can send an airplane. But you want to talk about, OK? Yeah. You, you had know-- you had gotten very good information that Eichmann was in Argentina, was in Buenos Aires.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if it was good or not. After we captured him, we knew that's good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Turned out you knew it was good.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, we didn't know in the beginning. But we got an information that is-- as I know, that it is, it was Fritz Bauer, the prosecutor of Hessen in Germany-- Jewish-- that he got a letter from a man called Hermann-- half Jew and half blind-- that his daughter is dating a man called Eichmann, a young man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now it-- he heard it from [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the media through the radio that was the Nuremberg trial. And he heard suddenly that Eichmann is not there. And he-- and it was Sicily. I think his deputy of Eichmann was there. And I said it's not my-- I'm not guilty, that my, my boss was Eichmann. So where is he? Nobody knew where he is. So I don't want to go back. Only if you ask me how it was escaped, that's another story. You want me to tell it now? Sure, certainly.

speaker: interviewee
text: So let's finish only that through the Nuremberg trial, when he heard it, Eichmann, he remembered that his boy is dating-- his girl is dating a man called Eichmann. He said, maybe his father is the, is the guy, is the man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now how, how he was escape? How? He was escaped by-- after the war, he ran away. And the Americans arrested him. And he was arrested. And they asked his name. And  you said, who are you? And he said, I'm, I'm Eichmann. He was not a liar, whatever you will say about him. He was a kind of, you know, sending people to death, but not a liar. He said, I'm Eichmann. And the American said, you're Mr. Eckman? He didn't say Eichmann, because the Americans don't say Eckman.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said yes. I'm going to teach him how to spell it. And he looked in his books. He didn't say Eckman. I said, OK, go to the prison. They took him prison. And he dressed himself as an, as an aviator. And he ran away from prison. And then the Franciscan, the German church, furnished him with the, all the papers and send him to Argentina. So they had to do something with the American with the Eckman or Eichmann. And then they helped him, the church. The-- you should know the Franciscan, the German church, helped a lot of Nazis to escape South America.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he had made his way to, to South America and stayed there, obviously?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [INAUDIBLE]. And his family was there-- three children, I think, three or four, and the small boy of six years old, that now it was discovered suddenly that he is a, he's a-- became a professor in Bonn. And he was then six years old. Had another boy. And he lived very poorly. And it was not understood how a man like him could be a billionaire if he would take only the money to leave some Jews alive. But it looks like it was very important for him to kill. And also, how you say it, didn't mind the money, it looks like. And he mind to stay alive with his family. Because he was really a family man, as we-- I tell it later.
